content.text: A tool marketplace security and compliance review is a systematic evaluation process applied to every tool candidate before it enters an enterprise's approved tool registry. It combines automated static and dynamic analysis, regulatory compliance mapping, and vendor due-diligence checks into a single governed assessment that produces a risk tier, a set of required compensating controls, and a binary approve/conditional/reject decision.

The review applies to any tool that an AI agent might invoke: third-party API SDKs, open-source libraries exposing tool interfaces, internal microservice connectors, and SaaS integration plugins. The output is a structured security finding report that becomes part of the tool's permanent registry record and is re-evaluated on a scheduled cadence.

AI agents can invoke tools at machine speed, with no human in the loop to catch an unexpected behavior. A tool that exfiltrates data, calls undisclosed third-party sub-processors, or escalates permissions silently creates a compliance breach that may not surface until an audit. The marketplace security review is the pre-production control that catches these issues before agents have access.

Regulators increasingly expect evidence that AI systems have gone through formal security evaluations. The EU AI Act and NIST AI RMF both require documentation of controls over third-party component risk. A structured review process with a documented output satisfies this requirement and reduces audit preparation time significantly.
Four evaluation layers—static, dynamic, compliance, vendor—ensure no risk dimension is skipped.
Tools receive a Low/Medium/High/Critical tier that determines monitoring intensity and re-review frequency.
Review reports serve as evidence artifacts for SOC 2, ISO 27001, and regulatory audits.
Developers can consume marketplace tools with confidence that security has already evaluated them.
High-risk tools receive additional runtime controls (call-rate limits, output scrubbing) while low-risk tools enter the marketplace with minimal friction.
A full four-layer review can take two to four weeks for high-risk tools, creating tension with fast-moving development cycles.
Effective dynamic analysis and vendor due diligence require security engineers with specific tool-testing skills, which are scarce in many organisations.
Some vendors refuse to share penetration test certificates or SOC 2 reports without NDAs, adding procurement overhead.
An approved tool version becomes a new risk surface after every major update. Version-pinning and update-triggered re-reviews are necessary but operationally demanding.
Each layer addresses a distinct risk category. Together they provide defence-in-depth across the tool supply chain.
Static analysis scans the tool's source code and dependency tree before runtime. This layer runs automatically on submission and blocks high-severity findings from advancing to subsequent stages.
Semgrep or CodeQL rules scan tool wrapper code for injection patterns, hardcoded secrets, unsafe deserialization, and known insecure API usage.
Dependency scanning (Trivy, Snyk) identifies CVEs in transitive dependencies. Tools with CVSS 9.0+ findings are blocked pending remediation.
Trufflehog or Gitleaks scan commit history and source files for accidental credential inclusion.

Dynamic analysis runs the tool in a controlled sandbox environment and probes its runtime behavior. This catches issues invisible to static analysis: undocumented network calls, permission escalation attempts, and data handling behavior.
OWASP ZAP or Burp Suite intercepts traffic between the agent sandbox and the tool endpoint to identify unexpected data egress.
The tool is invoked with minimal credentials and the review team verifies it does not attempt privilege escalation or scope expansion.
Tool responses are inspected for unexpected data inclusions—PII from adjacent records, internal system metadata, or prompt injection payloads in returned content.
This layer maps the tool's data handling profile against applicable regulatory frameworks based on the data it accesses.
For third-party tools, the review includes evaluation of the vendor's security posture independent of the tool's own code quality.
Verify the vendor has a current (within 12 months) report covering the relevant trust service criteria.
Request evidence of annual third-party penetration testing of the tool's API endpoint.
Verify the vendor has a public VDP or bug bounty program and a documented response SLA.
Confirm the vendor's sub-processor list and verify each sub-processor meets the same data residency requirements.

attrs.alt: Tool marketplace security review layer diagram

content.text: Lawful basis, data minimisation, sub-processor DPA, right-to-erasure support
PHI boundary enforcement, BAA with vendor, audit log retention
Cardholder data isolation, network segmentation, TLS version requirements
Availability SLA, access control evidence, incident response documentation
Consumer opt-out support, data sale disclosure, retention schedule
Every tool entering the marketplace for the first time.
Any tool update that changes the dependency tree, permission scopes, or network surface.
All approved tools on a schedule proportional to their risk tier: High-risk every 90 days, Medium every 180 days, Low every 365 days.
Any tool involved in a security incident undergoes an expedited full re-review before re-approval.

Stand up a tool security review pipeline as a CI/CD workflow triggered by tool submission. Integrate automated scan stages before any human reviewer touches the request. Treat the review report as a versioned artifact stored alongside the tool registry entry.
Create a tool-review repository where wrapper code and dependency manifests are submitted as pull requests.
Wire CI pipelines to run SAST (Semgrep), SCA (Trivy), and secrets scanning (Trufflehog) on every PR automatically.
Gate PRs on scan results: block merge if CVSS 9.0+ is found or secrets are detected.
On passing static checks, provision an ephemeral sandbox environment via Terraform and run DAST and permission probe tests.
Route the automated scan report plus intake form to the security review board ticket for human compliance and vendor review.
Publish approved tools to the registry with full metadata, scan report links, and review expiry date.

import subprocess, json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from pathlib import Path

class FindingSeverity(str, Enum):
    LOW = "low"
    MEDIUM = "medium"
    HIGH = "high"
    CRITICAL = "critical"

@dataclass
class SecurityFinding:
    rule_id: str
    severity: FindingSeverity
    description: str
    location: str

@dataclass
class StaticScanResult:
    tool_id: str
    sast_findings: list[SecurityFinding] = field(default_factory=list)
    sca_findings: list[SecurityFinding] = field(default_factory=list)
    secrets_found: bool = False

    @property
    def blocks_approval(self) -> bool:
        """True if any finding severity blocks automatic approval."""
        critical_sast = any(
            f.severity in (FindingSeverity.HIGH, FindingSeverity.CRITICAL)
            for f in self.sast_findings
        )
        critical_sca = any(
            f.severity == FindingSeverity.CRITICAL
            for f in self.sca_findings
        )
        return critical_sast or critical_sca or self.secrets_found

def run_semgrep_scan(source_dir: Path) -> list[SecurityFinding]:
    """Run Semgrep SAST and parse findings."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["semgrep", "--config=auto", "--json", str(source_dir)],
        capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=120
    )
    findings = []
    for item in json.loads(result.stdout).get("results", []):
        sev_map = {"ERROR": FindingSeverity.HIGH, "WARNING": FindingSeverity.MEDIUM,
                   "INFO": FindingSeverity.LOW}
        findings.append(SecurityFinding(
            rule_id=item["check_id"],
            severity=sev_map.get(item["extra"]["severity"], FindingSeverity.LOW),
            description=item["extra"]["message"],
            location=f"{item['path']}:{item['start']['line']}",
        ))
    return findings

def run_trivy_scan(image_or_path: str) -> list[SecurityFinding]:
    """Run Trivy SCA and parse CVE findings."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["trivy", "fs", "--format=json", image_or_path],
        capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=180
    )
    findings = []
    for vuln in json.loads(result.stdout).get("Results", [{}])[0].get("Vulnerabilities", []):
        sev_map = {"CRITICAL": FindingSeverity.CRITICAL, "HIGH": FindingSeverity.HIGH,
                   "MEDIUM": FindingSeverity.MEDIUM, "LOW": FindingSeverity.LOW}
        findings.append(SecurityFinding(
            rule_id=vuln.get("VulnerabilityID", "UNKNOWN"),
            severity=sev_map.get(vuln.get("Severity", "LOW"), FindingSeverity.LOW),
            description=vuln.get("Description", "")[:120],
            location=vuln.get("PkgName", ""),
        ))
    return findings

# Usage
scan = StaticScanResult(tool_id="stripe-python-v7")
scan.sast_findings = run_semgrep_scan(Path("./tools/stripe-wrapper"))
scan.sca_findings = run_trivy_scan("./tools/stripe-wrapper")
print(f"Blocks approval: {scan.blocks_approval}")
print(f"SAST findings: {len(scan.sast_findings)}, SCA findings: {len(scan.sca_findings)}")

A healthcare technology company maintains a tool marketplace for its clinical AI agents. Every tool submission triggers a GitHub Actions workflow that runs Semgrep against the tool wrapper code, Trivy against the requirements.txt, and Trufflehog against the commit history. CRITICAL findings automatically close the PR with a bot comment citing the blocking finding. For tools that pass static checks, a Terraform module provisions an ephemeral AWS sandbox account where a DAST script runs traffic interception using mitmproxy. The compliance team then manually reviews the traffic log against a HIPAA PHI boundary checklist before the tool can be published. Tools that handle patient data require a BAA signature from the vendor before the compliance mapping step can close.
Running only SAST and calling it done:
Dynamic analysis and vendor due diligence catch entire risk categories that static analysis misses.
Always request the actual SOC 2 report, not just the vendor's claims page.
A tool's direct dependencies may be clean while its transitive graph contains critical CVEs. SCA must scan the full dependency tree.
Approving version 1.0.0 and then allowing agents to auto-upgrade to 1.2.0 without a new review defeats the purpose of the process.
Treating the review as a one-time gate:
Threat landscapes evolve. New CVEs appear in previously approved tools. Scheduled re-reviews are not optional.
Human reviewers should only see findings summaries, not raw tool output. Automation reduces reviewer fatigue and processing time.
Publish scan reports to the registry:
Developers and auditors should be able to access the original scan report for any approved tool version.
Specify in policy which finding severities auto-block, which require compensating controls, and which are informational.
Critical-tier tools re-review every 60 days; Low-tier every 365 days. Match review effort to actual risk exposure.
Include runtime monitoring as a compensating control:
For tools approved at Medium risk, require anomaly detection on tool call patterns as a condition of approval.

As the tool count grows, the review pipeline must scale without adding linear headcount. Parallelising the four review layers (running static, dynamic, and vendor outreach simultaneously rather than sequentially) cuts median review time by 50-60%. A risk-scoring pre-triage model that auto-approves clearly low-risk tools (read-only, no external egress, no PII scope) reduces human review volume significantly.
Group re-reviews by expiry date into weekly batches to avoid on-call burden.
Tools sharing the same underlying library (e.g., requests, boto3) share CVE findings. A shared library approval registry avoids re-evaluating the same CVE for every tool.
Provide tool owners a findings dashboard with actionable remediation guidance so they can fix issues before the human review stage.
Open-source SAST with an extensive rule library for Python, JavaScript, Go, and Java tool wrappers.
Fast, comprehensive SCA tool supporting container images, file systems, and Git repositories.
Secrets scanning tool with support for 700+ credential formats across Git history and file systems.
Industry-standard DAST tools for web API scanning in sandbox environments.
Open-source traffic interception proxy for capturing and inspecting tool network calls in sandbox environments.
Compliance automation platforms that track SOC 2 evidence and vendor certification expiry dates.

The review pipeline itself is a high-value target: compromising it allows attackers to insert malicious tools into the approved registry. Protect it by storing scan tools as pinned Docker images in a private registry, signing scan reports with a known key, and requiring two-reviewer sign-off for any changes to the review pipeline's CI configuration. The approved tool registry should be read-only from agent runtimes and writable only by the automated pipeline with human final approval.

Reliability of the review pipeline is also critical. A scan tool outage should block approvals, not silently skip them. Implement health checks on all scan tools and alert on scan-stage failures. Approved tools should not be auto-removed from the registry on pipeline failure—only new approvals should require a healthy pipeline.
A four-layer security review (static, dynamic, compliance, vendor) provides systematic coverage of tool risk across the supply chain.
Automated scanning stages reduce human review burden and accelerate approval cycles without sacrificing coverage.
Compliance mapping must be driven by the data classification of what the tool accesses, not just the tool's own claims.
Approved tools require periodic re-review proportional to their risk tier; approval is not permanent.
Protecting the review pipeline itself is as important as the reviews it produces.
